title: Choosing a Branching Strategy
description:
## Which Workflow Fits?

A small team practices **continuous delivery**: they want to integrate work into `main` many times a day, keep `main` always releasable, and avoid long-lived branches that drift and cause painful merges. They rely on feature flags and a strong automated test suite.

Which branching strategy best fits these goals?

options.text: **Trunk-based development** — everyone commits to (or merges very short-lived branches into) `main` frequently, guarded by CI and feature flags
A single shared branch where each developer keeps their own personal long-running fork merged once per release
One permanent feature branch per developer that is only merged at the end of the project

explanation: **Trunk-based development** is designed exactly for fast, continuous delivery: developers integrate into `main` (the trunk) at least daily using short-lived branches or direct commits, keeping the integration cost low and `main` always releasable. Feature flags hide unfinished work and a strong CI suite catches regressions. **Gitflow** introduces several long-lived branches (`develop`, release, hotfix) and a heavier release ceremony — it suits scheduled, versioned releases, not many-times-a-day delivery. Long-running personal forks or per-developer permanent branches (c, d) maximize divergence and lead to large, risky merges — the opposite of continuous integration.

hints.title: What hurts continuous delivery?
hints.body: Long-lived branches drift from main and make merges large and risky. The strategy that minimizes branch lifetime aligns best with integrating many times a day.
